Most companies no longer care whether you memorized obscure JavaScript trivia. They care whether you can reason through unfamiliar code, debug production issues, and make sound engineering decisions.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The JavaScript Interview Has Changed
A few years ago, preparing for a JavaScript interview often meant memorizing lists.
What is hoisting?
Explain closures.
What’s the difference between == and ===?
How does the prototype chain work?
Developers filled notebooks with definitions, watched “Top 100 JavaScript Interview Questions” videos, and hoped enough trivia would stick before interview day.
That approach worked — until it didn’t.
Today, AI can answer nearly every syntax question in seconds. If an interviewer asks, “How do you flatten an array?” almost anyone can generate the answer instantly. Hiring managers know this. They know that memorizing language features is no longer a competitive advantage.
